questions.question: Explain the five scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization - percentage of time CPU is busy
Throughput - number of processes/threads completed per unit time
Turnaround Time - time from submission to completion
Waiting Time - total time spent in ready queue
Response Time - time from request to first execution response

questions.question: Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads.
keyPoints: User-level: managed by library in user space, faster creation/switch
Kernel-level: OS-managed, true parallelism on multiprocessors
User-level limitation: blocking call blocks entire process
Kernel-level: any thread can block without affecting others

questions.question: Describe the Round Robin scheduling algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: Time quantum allocated to each thread in circular order
Thread preempted when quantum expires, moved to queue back
Example: T1(10ms), T2(5ms), T3(8ms) with quantum=4ms
T1 runs 4ms, T2 runs 4ms, T3 runs 4ms, T1 runs remaining 6ms, etc.

questions.question: What is thread context switching? How does its overhead compare to process context switching?
keyPoints: Thread context switch saves/restores registers, PC, stack pointer
Does NOT need to change memory management information
Process context switch must flush TLB, update memory structures
Thread switch is faster because address space remains same

questions.question: Explain the three multithreading models with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Many-to-One: many user threads → one kernel thread - blocks if any blocks
One-to-One: one user thread → one kernel thread - true parallelism, resource heavy
Many-to-Many: many user threads → many kernel threads - best of both worlds

questions.question: What is thread affinity? Why is it important in multiprocessor systems?
keyPoints: Thread affinity binds thread to specific CPU core(s)
Improves cache performance - data stays in core's local cache
Types: soft (hint) and hard (mandatory)
Reduces cache misses and memory bandwidth pressure

questions.question: Compare FCFS and SJF scheduling algorithms.
keyPoints: FCFS: First-Come-First-Served, non-preemptive, simple but convoy effect
SJF: Shortest Job First, selects smallest burst time, optimal average waiting
SJF disadvantages: starvation of long processes, requires burst time prediction
Both can be preemptive (SRTF) or non-preemptive

questions.question: Explain Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling algorithm. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and how it can be implemented in practice.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Select thread with smallest burst time from ready queue
Non-preemptive SJF: once running, cannot be preempted
Preemptive SJF (SRTF): can preempt if new shorter thread arrives
Advantage: Minimum average waiting time (proven mathematically)
Disadvantages: Starvation of long processes, requires burst time prediction
Implementation: Use past behavior, user input, or statistical estimates
Aging can address starvation by gradually increasing priority of waiting processes

questions.question: Discuss thread scheduling in symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) systems. What are the challenges and solutions?
keyPoints: SMP: Multiple CPUs share common memory, each can run any thread
Challenges: Cache coherence, load balancing, thread affinity
Approaches: Common ready queue (simple but bottleneck) vs per-CPU queues
Cache affinity: Prefer running thread on core where its data is cached
Load balancing: Migrate threads from busy to idle cores
NUMA considerations: Keep threads near their memory nodes

questions.question: Explain the multilevel queue scheduling algorithm and its implementation.
keyPoints: Multiple ready queues for different process/thread types
Each queue can have different scheduling algorithm
Example: System queue (FCFS), Interactive queue (RR), Batch queue (FCFS)
Fixed priority between queues - system processes always first
Time slicing between queues - give all queues some CPU time
Advantages: Handles diverse workload types efficiently
Disadvantage: May cause starvation of lower priority queues

questions.question: Compare preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Discuss scenarios where each is appropriate.
keyPoints: Preemptive: OS can forcibly remove CPU from running thread
Non-preemptive: Thread runs until it yields or blocks
Preemptive advantages: Better response time, prevents monopoly of CPU
Preemptive disadvantages: More context switches, overhead
Non-preemptive advantages: Simpler, less overhead, good for critical tasks
Non-preemptive appropriate: Simple RTOS, kernel operations, device drivers
Preemptive appropriate: Time-sharing, interactive systems, desktop OS
